speaker: interviewer
text: Tape six, December 11, 1995. We're interviewing Mendel Flaster, in San Diego. The interview is in English. We were talking about the day you were liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Well, early morning. Two Jeeps rode in, in the camp. The SS was already away. And when the soldiers came in, and they went down from their Jeeps. And they see what's happened in that camp, they were standing like statues for at least 15 to 20 minutes. The tears were rolling down their cheeks, crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as we came to them and-- and-- and pulled them and kissed their hands and their feet. And that they were later, you know, come to themselves and start talking to us in English, what we didn't understood. But we could imagine what they're saying. They hold us, you know? And they shaked hands with us. And they told us that they will come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: About a-- not quite a half hour, the whole brass came in, generals, photographers. With their thing. And they start making pictures with the whole camp. They didn't believe their eyes, what they're seeing. The dead people were piled up as high as the barracks. In the barracks, they couldn't go in. It was just unbelievable. They ran out. You know, they sent a-- a company to catch the SS. And sure enough, about a day later, they brought back all the SS, what ran away.

speaker: interviewer
text: They brought them back. And they put them to work right away, to dig holes and to bury the dead people. Because they know that that camp will be a-- be a-- a catastrophe, you know? It--

speaker: interviewee
text: A sickness will break out. They know that. They brought in, right away, food for us. They gived us cans of-- one can was of meat, you know, conserved meat. And one can was of cookies. Then I remember, like it will be yesterday, when I open a can and start eating that meat, the pain was unbearable. I couldn't eat. I put it away. Open the other can. The same thing. Mine stomach was just killing me. I put it away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember that my mother, God bless her soul, that from home, when a child was sick, a cold or something, in Poland, it was not like a doctor see them, or right away you run to a doctor, take medicines. Was-- was home remedies. So my mother cooked tea. You know? Hot water, add a little bit of tea. And that was the remedy. With lemon, you know. So I took a little bit, water and made a little bit of fire and cooked hot water. And I drank that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I felt as like you have a burn, and you put something to cool it. And I felt that, that hot water is helping me. So I was drinking hot water and told to all my friends to do the same thing. Because people were-- were eating this, getting diarrhea, and dying, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the English liberated us. They're seeing where people, after liberation, people were dying in the thousands. In the thousands, every day.

speaker: interviewee
text: They brought in the Red Cross. They start looking. And finally, they went into the kitchen. They found stale bread with flour that underlies that. And they see that that was what the trouble is. That that was poison.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they made a hole in the ground. They buried all the bread with the flour, everything. And they start giving us strict diets and taking out people from the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they took the barracks where the SS were living before. They made a hospital over there. And they took all the people that were still to help, took them over there, and give them vitamins with strict diet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Me, as I was sick myself, but I volunteer to go over there to that place, to help the people. Can you imagine? How my mind was working always to help other people.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the thing is, I want you to understand, that I helped other people. But that way I was able to help myself. First, to go out from the camp. Because that camp was-- I know it was not good. But here I will stay, I die. Second, I helped other people. I wash them. I give them food. Whatever had to be done. I took them to the toilet. Many of them couldn't walk. I walk them to the-- the toilet, you know, things like that. But over there were vitamins, that I could eat as many as I want. The vitamins was big white pills like this. Vitamins, that's what we needed, what we didn't have all the years. I eat the vitamins and I eat the soup, what they were cooking. And I helped other people. And I helped myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much did you weigh then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I weighed 72 pounds when I was liberated. I was a young man of 25.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many camps had you been in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fourteen. Fourteen camps. Six years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after you were working in the hospital, then--

speaker: interviewee
text: After I were working in the hospital. So finally they brought in all kind of Red Cross nurses, what that didn't need us. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went out. But they sent us to Lingen and to [INAUDIBLE]. You know, that's deep in Germany, to a-- a sanatorium, what we-- we recuperate over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was over there several weeks. I had come back to my strength. I came back to the camp here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I didn't like, too much anymore, Bergen-Belsen. Too much memories. And to be frank with you, I right away didn't like the English. You know? They were too strict, right away with us, you know? They're-- they're you do this. you do-- you cannot do this. You can-- you know? And i didn't like it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt that I'm liberated. I want to be free. So I took my friends. And I went from Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: I walked into-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what is the city before Hanover?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, to a city over there. And from that city, I took the train, what I went to Hanover. From Hanover to Diessen-- to-- to Giessen. And from Giessen, to Frankfurt am Main. And in Frankfurt am Main, I came-- so over there was the American sector. In the-- this first presence, they occupied a hotel. A hotel was bombard-- you know, half of it was damaged. But the other half was still living-- livingable-- you know, you could live in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went in, in that hotel. And I right away introduced myself. I am so and so, that and that, from that and that camp. And I got, right away, there were my friends, with me. So I said, what is the score? He said, the score is we have to wait and see what that is, what we can go to different countries, or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in the meantime, I have to eat. And we didn't have-- you know, we had the little bit what the soldiers brought in to eat, and hey, but that wasn't enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I found a solution. I went to the American kitchen. I already had experience with kitchens, you know? I went to the American kitchen. I said to the-- to the cook over there, I said to him, listen.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are here, people that are displaced persons. We need food. I don't want you to take away from the soldiers, nothing. Whatever is left over, please, give it to me. He looked at me and said, with pleasure. It was a-- a Jewish man, what he was in charge of the kitchen. He said, believe me, you will have so much food that you won't be able to eat. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and he did that. You know? Right away, you know, he took whatever he had over there, bread and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: He load me up a Jeep. And he said, take it. Take a soldier, and said go with him back to over there, to the hotel. And load this down. I brought back the food, right away, you know. It was what to eat. And I went over there twice a day, what after lunch and after dinner, that he give me food. He give me enough, what there was for breakfast, too. And we had what to eat. But it start coming, so many people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because we went to every train, to find people what have nowhere to go. We took them in to that hotel. We took them in. We give them a bed or a room to sleep in there, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And to eat. Because nobody had nothing, where to go. And we did this. And I was in charge of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Finally, it was so filled up, the hotel, that we didn't have any room. So we found a place in Salzheim, by Frankfurt am Main that over there, we built a camp. Barracks with beds from the UNRRA. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got the Jewish committee, what that worked on it. And the-- more people came into the thing, that we pushed them over there to the camp. And over there with-- you know. And I was one of the first five policemen in that camp. And we start having a-- over there, a kitchen. And later, packages came in from the UNRRA, what was all kind of meat and chocolate and candies and-- and coffee and things like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What it was, 10-pound packages. Everybody got a 10-pound package. You know? It was enough to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the kitchen was cooking, you know, what they got. And they cooked, over there, lunches and suppers. You know, it was very good food. People did not have to die. They ate. They had what to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I myself, I went to the German office over there. I didn't have to wear-- you know, I still had my-- mine clothes from the-- from the concentration camp. I told them I wanted-- everything was on cards, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And just after the war I said to them, I wanted a card for a suit, for pants and shoes. Well, I needed to wear something. They say they don't have it, blah, blah, blah. I then went over the-- the thing I said to him, listen. If I were to find a store, where that is, you don't give me the card. I will take it much more than the card will allow me. Because I'm entitled to it. You took it away from me. And don't give me any of that thing. He was afraid. [LAUGHS] He give me a card. Not only for me. I got cards for all my friends. And everybody got clothes to wear. And-- and shirts and suits and shoes and everything. We got everything. Because I did not let them get away with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see, I was already in charge of some of it. I was in the power. Because I had the American behind me. You see? So I wasn't afraid for them. They were afraid for me. So and in that camp-- What was the name of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Salzheim by Frankfurt am Main.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In a short time, that camp had 3,500 people, men, women, and children. I was working in that camp as a police officer. Later I got sent from the American CIC. They sent me to Stuttgart, to a school. I was schooled over there as a police officer. And later I got promoted, right away, to a sergeant. I went back to Stuttgart, for another promotion to that-- then I got promoted, that I was investigator. I investigate big crimes. Only the world criminals what I was after, and murder and rape. That was my thing to investigate. And I was very good at it. I can tell you as I was, all the years, very, you know, for humanity. But I was against breaking the laws, I was against, you know, the blockaltestes. I put away many of them, what they didn't see the daylight anymore. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any in particular that we would know, their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were blockaltestes, you see? Well, what that you didn't know. They were kapos. One SS man, what I personally caught from the-- when at Auschwitz, SS man. I personally caught him. I recognized him. Because he used to come on the Baustelle, where-- where I was working. But he was a friend of my kapo. So he came--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I know his first name was Hans. But I don't remember the second name. He was a tall man, and I recognized him in Lingen, in [INAUDIBLE] over there where I was. And at [INAUDIBLE], I recognized him over there. But I-- I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you do this job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I did this job till 1949. 1950, I came to United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife through my friends. You know, in 19-- beginning of 1948, we were know-- knowing each other for about seven, eight months. And we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: You met her in the-- the camp you were living in, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Salzheim. In Salzheim, I find her, after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1948, I met her. And in 1948, we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any children over there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd-- my oldest boy was born in Germany. Abie, he was born in Germany. In Frankfurt am Main, in Sachsenhausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you-- what brought you to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was very important in Germany. And I was-- had a very good job. But it was the talk of other-- another war, with the Russian, over the Berlin situation at that time. And I did not want to be in another war right there, the front line.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had three places to go. I wanted to go, actually, first to Israel. But the-- the English didn't let in the Jewish people in Israel. So I could go to Canada, Australia, or the United States. So I took the United States. Because I was working with the CIC, with the CID so close. We were close like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: The MP and the provost marshal, we all worked together. You know, I was very close with them. Day in and day out, we were going out to the operas together.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were going out to the-- the shopping, the-- the dancing, and then, you know, and for entertaining, and for business together. You know? So that-- so I was very close with them. They liked me a lot. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, I had-- I had the authority there. Later, what that no German policeman could even come close to me. I-- I arrested German police. I arrested MP. I arrested them. That authority, I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you came to the United States, did you continue to be a policeman? Or what--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My mistake was that I didn't learn the English language in-- in Germany. If I would know the English language, I would go in here, in the police. Because with my record, what I had from there, from the CIC and CID, I would be here, a big shot. But I didn't know the English language. And that was a-- a big minus for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go when you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Straight to San Diego.

speaker: interviewer
text: For-- why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my wife had here a brother before, a year before coming to San Diego. And also, the CIC, the Chief of the CIC told me, if you want to go to a nice little town, go to San Diego, California. And I was set on that, you know? But-- so two things, what brought me here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do to make a living when you came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came here I worked tailoring, in stores, several years. And later I had my own shop, as a tailor. I made men-- men's clothes, ladies' clothes. I was working. I was very successful in it. Very, very nice shop. On Ray Street in North Park, my shop. And after that I got tired of tailoring. I'd went in the liquor business. I sold my tailor shop. And I bought a liquor store. Went in the liquor business. I was several years in the liquor business. Later, I retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have more children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had, here, another son, Sammy, and a daughter, Helen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. By the name Renee and Marcie. Very lovely children. Very lovely family. I'm very proud. I'm a very proud man, with my family, and with my life, what I did. I can look back on my life, and I am a very happy man. And I'm very thankful to Almighty God, that God give me the wisdom to do the right thing at the right time. And that God guided me, always. That I never was selfish, always was for other people. And I am very grateful for that. Because in times like this, if you do the right thing at the right time, the right places, you have a good chance. And I did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Do you have any special message you'd like to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have a message that I wanted to leave. What-- I am doing this. I'm going to schools to speak. I'm going-- I do all kind of things, what I feel is to do for human being, that it will be a better world for our children and our grandchildren. I would like to see that there will be a better world, that will be peace in Israel, and peace of the world, that people will understand each other better. Instead to kill each other, to love each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Manny, can you tell us who are these beautiful people around you?

speaker: interviewee
text: These beautiful people is my family, my lovely family. That's my my wife. That's my oldest son. That's my daughter-in-law, and my other son, Sammy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us all their names? What is your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Geta.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abie, Shellie, and Sam.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my wife. Her name is Geta. And the picture was made in 1950. And you can see how beautiful she is, inside and outside. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was made in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is taken in 1950. GETA (WIFE): No. No, 19-- GETA (WIFE): (WHISPERS) 1988. 1980, I'm sorry. GETA (WIFE): (WHISPERS) '88. In 1980. This is my granddaughter's bat mitzvah, my older granddaughter, Renee's. And the people are, from left to right, is my daughter-in-law's parents, what are Charlotte and Jerry. Later is-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --Marcie, my granddaughter, and my granddaughter, Renee. And their mother and father, what is Shelley and Abie. And my wife, Geta, and myself, Manny.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter with her little dog. That's my daughter, Helen. And the little dog is Honey. And that was taken in 1994. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's my granddaughter, Marcie. It was taken in 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my older granddaughter, Renee. And that was taken, also, in 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is letters, what children sended to me from different schools, what I spoke to them. They are very, very nice. And they're very adorable. And I keep quite a bit of it. Because I wanted to remember where I was and to whom I spoke. And it is, to me, a very important thing, to have them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great.
